common.ogImageAlt: GridGen — a labeled coordinate grid laid over an image, exported as a PDF

home.title: GridGen — Add a Coordinate Grid to Any Image & Export as PDF
home.description: Free browser tool to overlay a labeled coordinate grid on any image — photo, map, battle map, or floor plan — and export a print-ready A4 PDF. No upload, no signup.
home.keywords: grid overlay, add grid to image, grid on image, grid drawing method, image to grid PDF, coordinate grid generator, add grid to map, battle map grid, cross stitch grid from photo, pixel art grid, coordinate plane grid, sports pitch zone grid, floor plan grid, reference grid photo, print grid PDF

wikiHow.title: How GridGen Works — User Guide & Wiki
wikiHow.description: A complete guide to GridGen: how to upload an image, configure a coordinate grid, customize its appearance, add chess or numeric labels, and export a print-ready PDF.
wikiHow.keywords: how to use the grid drawing method, add coordinate labels to image, grid overlay tutorial, chess A1 grid labels, coordinate grid PDF export, reference grid photo

wikiTech.title: GridGen Technical Reference — Stack & Architecture
wikiTech.description: How GridGen is built: a zero-dependency vanilla JavaScript app that renders a coordinate grid on an HTML5 Canvas and exports to PDF with jsPDF.

mapGrid.title: Add a Coordinate Grid to a Map & Export as PDF — GridGen
mapGrid.description: Lay a coordinate reference grid over a map image and export a print-ready PDF — useful for orienteering, scouting, and field reference. A visual grid overlay, not a georeferenced map generator.
mapGrid.keywords: add coordinate grid to map, overlay grid on map pdf, topographic map grid overlay, orienteering map grid, reference grid on map image, military-style map grid, print map with grid

useCases.title: GridGen Use Cases — Real-World Ways to Add a Grid to an Image
useCases.description: Practical ways to use GridGen: the grid drawing method, D&D battle maps, cross-stitch charts, coordinate-plane worksheets, orienteering maps, pitch zones, floor plans and more — each with the exact settings to use.
useCases.keywords: grid drawing method, battle map grid, cross stitch grid from photo, coordinate plane worksheet, orienteering map grid, pitch zone grid, floor plan grid overlay, escape room grid, photo composition grid, archaeology site grid, add grid to image use cases

featureList: Grid by number of cells (1–100 columns/rows) or by cell size in pixels (5–500)
Customizable line color, thickness (1–10 px), and opacity (10–100%)
Inside labels with a contrast halo, or outside labels in clean margins
Live preview that updates as you adjust the grid
Print-ready A4 PDF export, auto portrait/landscape, scaled to fit
Runs entirely in your browser — images are never uploaded
Free and open source (GPL v3), no signup

wikiHow.howtoName: How to add a coordinate grid to an image and export it as a PDF with GridGen

steps.name: Upload an image
steps.text: Drag and drop or click to select a PNG, JPG, or WEBP image.

steps.name: Choose a grid mode
steps.text: Define the grid by number of cells or by cell size in pixels.

steps.name: Customize appearance
steps.text: Set the line color, thickness, and opacity for readability.

steps.name: Add coordinate labels
steps.text: Pick chess or numeric labels, inside or outside placement, and the origin corner.

steps.name: Export the PDF
steps.text: Click Download PDF to save an A4 file at full image resolution.

faq.a: Yes. It is free, open source, and released under the GNU GPL v3.

faq.q: Do my images get uploaded anywhere?
faq.a: No. All processing happens locally in your browser. Your images are never sent to a server.
PNG, JPG, and WEBP — any raster format your browser can decode.

faq.q: What resolution is the exported PDF?
faq.a: The grid is composited at your image's full native resolution, then placed on an A4 page scaled to fit.
Any modern browser with HTML5 Canvas and File API support — Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge.

mapGrid.howtoName: How to add a coordinate grid to a map and export it as a PDF

steps.name: Get a base map
steps.text: Use a map image you already have — a screenshot, a scan, or an exported topographic map.

steps.name: Upload it to GridGen
steps.text: Drag the map into the preview area or click to select it.

steps.name: Set the grid
steps.text: Choose the number of squares or a fixed cell size to match the reference grid you need.

steps.name: Add coordinate labels
steps.text: Turn on chess (A1) or numeric (1,1) labels so every square has a reference.

steps.name: Export as PDF
steps.text: Download a print-ready PDF to take into the field.

faq.q: Does GridGen generate a topographic map or pull map data?
faq.a: No. GridGen overlays a grid on a map image you provide. To get a base map, take a screenshot of an online map, scan a paper map, or export one from a mapping service, then upload it here.

faq.q: Are the squares real UTM or MGRS coordinates?
faq.a: No. The grid is a visual reference — evenly spaced squares with A1 or numeric labels. They are not georeferenced to the map's projection, so treat them as a relative reference system, not survey-grade coordinates.

faq.q: Can I match the grid to an existing map grid?
faq.a: Partly — use By size mode and adjust the cell size so the overlay lines roughly align with the printed grid on your map image. It is a visual alignment, not a calculated one.
Yes. GridGen is free and open source, and your map never leaves your device — all processing happens in your browser.

faq.q: Do I need an account for any of these use cases?
faq.a: No. GridGen is free, needs no signup, and runs entirely in your browser — your image never leaves your device.

faq.q: Does the grid represent real-world coordinates?
faq.a: No. It is a visual reference grid of evenly spaced, labeled squares. It is not georeferenced and does not compute real UTM, MGRS, or Cartesian coordinates — treat it as a relative reference everyone can name.

faq.q: Can I use GridGen for the grid drawing method?
faq.a: Yes — that is the classic art use. Upload your reference, pick By cells (for example 10×10), add chess labels outside, and lower the line opacity. Print the PDF and draw the same grid on your canvas to copy or scale up the picture square by square.

faq.q: Can I make a D&D or TTRPG battle-map grid?
faq.a: Yes. Upload your map illustration, use By size so squares stay a fixed size, turn on chess (A1) labels, and place them inside so a reference like "C4" reads directly on the battlemat.

faq.q: Can I turn a photo into a cross-stitch or pixel-art chart?
faq.a: Yes — use By size so each cell maps to one stitch or pixel, and add labels to count rows and columns. GridGen overlays the counting grid only; it does not reduce colors or build a stitch palette.

faq.q: Can I make a coordinate-plane worksheet?
faq.a: Yes. Use By cells with numeric (1,1) labels and a bottom-left origin to match the Cartesian convention students see in textbooks, then print the PDF as a handout.

faq.q: Can I add a reference grid to a map?
faq.a: Yes — bring a map image and overlay a labeled grid for orienteering, scouting, or field reference. The squares are a relative reference, not real UTM or MGRS coordinates; the grids-on-maps page covers this in depth.

faq.q: Can I zone a sports pitch or a floor plan?
faq.a: Yes. Use By cells to divide a pitch or floor-plan image into labeled zones, and place labels outside so the underlying plan stays visible — handy for coaching briefings or venue wayfinding.

faq.q: Which settings should I use for my project?
faq.a: Use By cells for a fixed grid shape (art, worksheets, zones) and By size for a fixed square size (battle maps, counted-craft charts). Use chess (A1) labels to name squares, or numeric (1,1) labels for coordinate-plane work.
Yes. Every use case ends in a print-ready A4 PDF — orientation is chosen automatically and the grid is drawn at your image's full resolution.
